request.question: KOIL ran a radio show featuring Mark Levin who worked for which Attorney General?

title: George J. Terwilliger III
Mark Levin
WHLO
The Liberty Amendments
WNIX
Earth 2 (TV series)
KOIL
WWLZ
WTMA
The Mark Levin Show
sentences: George J. Terwilliger III (born June 5, 1950) is an American lawyer and politician.
He is a partner in the Washington, D.C. office of McGuireWoods LLP and is a former United States Deputy Attorney General and acting United States Attorney General.
Terwilliger, of Vermont, was nominated on February 14, 1992, by President George H.W. Bush to be Deputy Attorney General at the U.S. Department of Justice.
He would succeed William Pelham Barr.

As Deputy Attorney General, Terwilliger became the second-highest-ranking official in the United States Department of Justice (DOJ) and ran the day-to-day operations of the Department, serving in that position from 1991 through 1993.
He was appointed to the position after serving as the United States Attorney for the District of Vermont.
In addition, he currently serves on the Advisory Board of Intellaine, LLP, a U.S. defense and risk engineering firm located in Arlington, Virginia.
Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.
He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".
Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.
He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".
On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.
WHLO (640 AM) – branded 640 WHLO – is a commercial radio station licensed to Akron, Ohio, serving both the Akron and Canton metro areas.
Owned by iHeartMedia, the station broadcasts primarily a news/talk format.

WHLO is the local affiliate for Fox News Radio; conservative talk programs "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show", "The Mark Levin Show", and "The Schnitt Show"; and overnight night talk shows "The Dave Ramsey Show" and "Coast to Coast AM".
The station also serves as the radio home of the Kent State Golden Flashes.
The WHLO studios are located in North Canton, while the station transmitter resides in Copley.
In addition to a standard analog transmission, WHLO is available online via iHeartRadio.
The Liberty Amendments: Restoring the American Republic is a book by the American talk radio host and lawyer Mark Levin, published in 2013.
In it, Levin lays out and makes a case for eleven Constitutional amendments which he believes would restore the Constitution’s chief components: federalism, republicanism, and limited government.
WNIX (1330 AM) is a radio station broadcasting a News-Talk format, featuring Rush Limbaugh, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, Doug Stephan and Dave Ramsey.
Licensed to Greenville, Mississippi, USA, the station is currently owned by Delta Radio Network LLC.
Earth 2 is an American science fiction television series which aired on NBC from November 6, 1994 to June 4, 1995.
The show was canceled after one season of 22 episodes.

It follows the journey and settlement of a small expeditionary group called the Eden Project, with the intent to journey to an Earth-like planet called G889 in an attempt to find a cure to an illness called "the syndrome".

The series was created by Michael Duggan, Carol Flint, Mark Levin, and Billy Ray, produced by Amblin Entertainment and Universal Television, and filmed primarily in northern New Mexico around the Santa Fe area.
The series' music was composed by David Bergeaud, and the executive producers were Michael Duggan, Mark Levin, and Carol Flint.
KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.
It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.
KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.
It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team.
KOIL's studios are located at Dodge Street and 50th Avenue in Midtown Omaha, and its transmitter site is located in Bellevue, Nebraska.
KOIL operates at 5000 watts around the clock but at night it uses a directional antenna to protect other stations on 1290 kHz.
WWLZ (820 AM) is a radio station in the Elmira-Corning market in New York state.

It is a talk radio station that airs the Premiere Networks lineup ("Glenn Beck Radio Program", "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show" and "Coast to Coast AM") during the workday and overnight, Cumulus Media Networks programs ("First Light", "Imus in the Morning", "The Mark Levin Show", "The John Batchelor Show") during the evening and in morning drive, MRN races and Elmira Jackals hockey.
WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.
This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.
WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.
The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.
They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.
Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.
Each hour begins with Westwood One News.
The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.
The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".

The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.  He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

KOIL | KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.  It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.  KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.  It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team. The Mark Levin Show | The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.  The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".  The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

Mark Levine (journalist) | Mark H. Levine (born May 7, 1966) is the Democratic Delegate to the Virginia House of Delegates from Virginia's 45th District, which encompasses the eastern half of the City of Alexandria, some of the northern West End, parts of South Arlington, and the Alexandria portion of Fairfax County.  A Constitutional lawyer, Levine served as Legislative Counsel to Congressman Barney Frank (D-MA) from 2001 to 2003 and was an early advocate in the "Marriage Equality” movement.  Since 2003, Levine has hosted a nationally syndicated progressive public policy radio program and worked as a TV pundit.  In 2014, Levine ran in the Democratic Primary for Virginia's 8th Congressional District last year, a seat currently held by Rep. Don Beyer.

WCHM | WCHM (1490 AM) and 96.7 FM is a radio station broadcasting a News Talk Information format.  Licensed to Clarkesville, Georgia, USA.  The station is currently owned by WCHM Radio, LLC, an affiliated company of Batten Communications, inc. and features programming from Premiere Radio Networks and FOX News Radio.  Major shows include Rush Limbaugh, Glenn Beck, Mark Levin, Sean Hannity, Clark Howard, Michael Savage and Coast to Coast with George Noory.  The station also features local programming and University of Georgia sports programming.

Ketzel Levine | Ketzel Levine is an American radio journalist who began her broadcast career in 1974.  She joined National Public Radio (NPR) in 1977 and worked, variously, as the network's arts producer, sports director, features reporter and garden expert.  From 2000 through 2008, she was senior correspondent for the NPR program "Morning Edition".  At the end of that year, due to cutbacks at the network, Levine was laid off, while working on a documentary series about Americans coping with economic stress and job loss.  Her final NPR broadcast was about how she, herself, had just lost her job.

WTMA | WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.  This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.  WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.  The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.  They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.  Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.  Each hour begins with Westwood One News.

response.hop2_query: Mark Levin Attorney General he served as chief of staff for
hop2_docs: Mark Levin | Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.  He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".  Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.  He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

Kevin J. O'Connor (attorney) | Kevin J. O'Connor serves as general counsel at Point72 Asset Management.  Previously, he served as an attorney appointed by President George W. Bush and was unanimously confirmed by the United States Senate as Connecticut’s 48th United States Attorney in 2002.  From January to April 2006, O'Connor served as Associate Deputy Attorney General of the United States.  In 2007, O'Connor served as Chief of Staff to United States Attorney General Alberto R. Gonzales.  In 2008, O'Connor was unanimously confirmed as Associate Attorney General of the United States, the number three position at the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ), a post he held until 2009, when he left the DOJ to join the law firm of Bracewell and Giuliani.

David L. Goldfein | David Lee Goldfein (born 1959) is a General in the United States Air Force who currently serves as the Air Force Chief of Staff.  Before, he served as Air Force Vice Chief of Staff.  He was previously Director of the Joint Staff, a position within the Joint Chiefs of Staff who assists the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.  On April 26, 2016, it was announced that Goldfein was nominated to succeed General Mark Welsh as the 21st Chief of Staff of the United States Air Force.  His confirmation hearing took place on June 16, and he succeeded Welsh on July 1, two days after his confirmation.  Gen Goldfein is the second Jewish U.S. Air Force Chief of Staff, after Gen Norton Schwartz, USAF (Ret).
David Mark Cullen | Major General David Mark Cullen {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} is a senior British Army officer.  He served as the Assistant Chief of the General Staff from 2013 to 2015.

Henry Wells (general) | Lieutenant General Sir Henry Wells, {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} (22 March 1898 – 20 October 1973) was a senior officer in the Australian Army.  Serving as Chief of the General Staff from 1954 to 1958, Wells' career culminated with his appointment as the first Chairman, Chiefs of Staff Committee, a position marking him as the professional head of the Australian Military.  He served in this capacity from March 1958 until March 1959, when he retired from the army.

Stuart A. Levey | Stuart A. Levey was the first Under Secretary for Terrorism and Financial Intelligence within the United States Department of the Treasury.  He was sworn in on July 21, 2004 as a political appointee of President George W. Bush.  President Obama asked Levey to remain in his position and Levey was one of only a small number of Senate-confirmed Bush appointees to be held over.  Levey served until March 2011.  Levey played a central role in the efforts to combat North Korea's and Iran's allegedly illicit conduct in the international financial system.  Prior to his nomination, Levey served as the Principal Associate Deputy Attorney General at the U.S. Department of Justice.  He had previously served as an Associate Deputy Attorney General and as the Chief of Staff of the Deputy Attorney General.  He was succeeded by David S. Cohen.  In January 2012, Levey joined HSBC as the bank's Chief Legal Officer.

Mark A. Milley | Mark Alexander Milley (born June 20, 1958) is a United States Army officer.  A four-star general, he is the 39th Chief of Staff of the Army.  He previously served as the 21st commanding general of U.S. Army Forces Command from August 15, 2014 to August 9, 2015.
response.summary_2: Mark Levin worked for Attorney General Edwin Meese. Mark Levin is an American lawyer and radio personality who hosts the syndicated radio program "The Mark Levin Show."
